data.title: ICE Review: Canola Continues Ascent With Help From Comparable Oils
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--The ICE Futures canola market maintained positive momentum, aided by rising comparable oils.

Chicago soyoil, European rapeseed and Malaysian palm oil were all higher. Crude oil also made gains despite a buildup of U.S. inventories.

Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada raised its estimates for both 2024-25 and 2025-26 canola stocks by 50,000 tons at 1.3 million and one million tons, respectively. The looming threat of tariffs on Canadian canola also prompted increased buying activity.

At mid-afternoon, the Canadian dollar was up two-tenths of a U.S. cent compared to Wednesday's close.

There were 43,197 contracts traded on Thursday, which compares with Wednesday when 54,224 contracts changed hands. Spreading accounted for 31,526 of the contracts traded.

 
Settlement prices are in Canadian dollars per metric ton.

data.title: New Zealand's Fonterra expects to meet upper half of fiscal 2025 forecast, hints at strong dividend
data.description:
New Zealand's Fonterra said on Friday that it expects earnings to meet the upper half of its forecast range of 40 to 60 New Zealand cents per share for the fiscal 2025.

The dairy giant expects to be in a position to pay a strong interim dividend.

The dairy cooperative has also raised its projection for milk collection up to 1.51 billion kilograms of milk solids(kgMS) for the current season, citing favourable weather conditions.

"Fonterra's earnings and the forecast Farmgate Milk Price have both benefitted from solid demand for our high value Ingredients products, and our sales book is well contracted for the season," said Chief Executive Officer Miles Hurrell.

data.title: Crombie REITs Brief: Keeps Outperform, But Target Edged Up To C$16.50 From $16 at National Bank On Q4 2024 "Strong occupancy and renewal spreads"
U.S. Farm Bankruptcies Trend Higher — Market Talk
data.description:
1513 ET - Some 55% more U.S. farmers declared bankruptcy in 2024 versus the previous year, although the number is still relatively low versus what was seen in the previous decade. In 2024, 216 farm bankruptcy cases were filed, says the American Farm Bureau Federation in a note, up from 139 bankruptcies in 2023. But despite the big uptick, the total number of bankruptcies is still well below the 599 bankruptcies reported in 2019 — a high point in bankruptcy cases before the disruption of the Covid-19 pandemic. But the trend is worrying, says the AFBF, especially as the USDA forecasts a decrease of crop receipts in 2025. "As the farm economy continues to be squeezed by decreased receipts and increased expenses, farm financials will be strained," says Samantha Ayoub of the AFBF. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1456 ET - Consumers shouldn't bank on BOGO deals for Tide laundry products or Charmin toilet paper anytime soon. Procter & Gamble CFO Andre Schulten says at the Consumer Analyst Group of New York conference that the company will "use promotion to drive incremental visibility of new innovation in store." At the same time, though, P&G will be careful not to reduce the perceived value of its product with heavy discounts. "That doesn't grow markets, it doesn't grow categories and it doesn't grow our share," Schulten says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1445 ET - Walmart's warning of slower sales gains, together with slowdown comparable sales growth during the holiday quarter, is sparking concerns on Wall Street. The retail giant's 4.6% growth in U.S. comparable sales in the fourth quarter from a 5.3% increase in the third quarter shows lower prices haven't been enough to drive sentiment higher among Americans. "We're growing profit faster than sales," CEO Douglas McMillon says on a call with analysts, noting that Walmart has runway to scale our higher-margin businesses like membership, marketplace and advertising. Its wholesale business, Sam's Club, also saw a sequential deceleration in comparable sales in the period, driving Costco Wholesale shares 2.4% lower. Shares of Amazon, Target and Kroger are down between 1.5% and 2%, while Walmart drops 6.5%. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1435 ET - U.S. farmers are expected to plant more acres of corn, at the expense of soybean, wheat, cotton and grain sorghum, CoBank said. Tanner Ehmke, grains and oilseeds economist with CoBank, said "While farmers tend to stick to historical crop rotations for agronomic reasons and market diversification, corn's price rally relative to other crops suggests a major shift in acreage is in the offing." A report from CoBank's Knowledge Exchange says corn acres are expected to climb 4.2%, to 94.5 million in 2025. "However, the potential for trade disputes with Canada and Mexico could significantly weaken the demand for U.S. corn and curb the increase in planted acreage," CoBank said. CoBank is a cooperative bank serving industries across rural America. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1431 ET - Procter & Gamble CFO Andre Schulten isn't sure the company will hit its fiscal 2025 outlook. The consumer-products company was banking on the second half of its fiscal year to be stronger than the first, but the macro environment continues to be challenging and has yet to fundamentally improve, Schulten says at the Consumer Analyst Group of New York conference. Tariffs, both enacted and proposed, are introducing new layers of volatility, he adds. The company is taking steps to support the low end of its outlook, but "there is some level of probability that external factors may lead to a slightly below guidance range outcome on the bottom line," Schulten says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1417 ET - St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem says that signs from interest-rate-sensitive sectors make it clear that the Fed's policy stance is still restricting the economy after a percentage point of interest-rate cuts in 2024. Slow activity in the housing sector and other industries that rise and fall with financing costs show that monetary policy remains at least a bit tight, Musalem says. "Low- and moderate-income households, and small businesses are still feeling the effects of interest rates and financing conditions," Musalem says. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1412 ET - Hasbro's licensing business is a huge margin driver that's relatively unexposed to the "tariff drama" spurred by the White House, CEO Chris Cocks says on a call with analysts. The toymaker's licensing unit has grown by 60% in the last three years, making Hasbro the third largest entertainment licensor globally, and the biggest in the digital games space, Cocks says. Hasbro plans to lean into licensing, and particularly into joint ventures and partnerships for its self-published portfolio in the years to come, the CEO says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1344 ET - Hasbro says half of its U.S. toy and game volume originates from China, but the company is working to cut that contribution down to under 40% over the next two years, CFO Gina Goetter says on a call with analysts. The shift comes as the company maneuvers to deal with Trump's new tariffs on Chinese imports and potential levies on imports from Mexico and Canada. Goetter says the company doesn't source from Canada and has minimal sourcing from Mexico. "We're really watching that China rate, that's what we baked in," the CFO says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1327 ET - There are compelling reasons for the Bank of Canada to cut again in March, says National Bank Financial economist Taylor Schleich. Traders have scaled back expectations for a 7th straight rate cut next month, with only one in three believing it will happen. Solid hiring, firmer inflation and less fear about hefty tariffs from President Trump are pushing people toward a March pause. Schleich says slack remains in the economy. As for hiring, Schleich says the job vacancy rate is at a 7-year low and the less timely Canada nonfarm payroll data suggests hiring remains tepid. Finally, Schleich says Trump's tariff threats alone are impactful, citing surveys from BOC and other business groups indicating executives are deferring investment, halting hiring plans, and mulling a move to the US. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1326 ET - St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem, a voter on the central bank's policy committee this year, says monetary policy is less restrictive than it was before last year's rate cuts, but is still working to restrain the economy modestly. Speaking at an Economic Club of New York meeting in Manhattan, Musalem says that it will be appropriate to keep policy restrictive if inflation stays above the Fed's 2% target. On the other hand, lower rates could be the right move is the labor market worsens and inflation expectations stay anchored, he adds. He presents an optimistic scenario as his baseline: "Looking ahead, I expect inflation will continue to converge to the FOMC's 2% target and the labor market will remain near full employment," he says, referring to the Federal Open Markets Committee that sets monetary policy. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1254 ET - Mexico's reliance on U.S. piped gas will continue to grow, Fitch Ratings says, even as uncertainties over bilateral trade relations increase. U.S. gas imports provide reliable and cost-effective energy for Mexico, buoyed by rising demand, constrained domestic production, and pipeline infrastructure expansion, Fitch says. The market share of U.S. piped gas in Mexico is expected to increase in the coming years, according to Fitch. Trade uncertainties have emerged since President Trump came into office, but the administration has mainly focused on Mexico's exports to the U.S., and Fitch says it sees policy-driven disruptions to the gas trade as a smaller risk. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1219 ET - Walmart set out a 3% to 4% revenue growth target for 2025 that would imply continued share gains given overall retail sales growth is expected to be more modest, Fitch Ratings' David Silverman says in a research note. Those gains would come despite Walmart's already massive scale and be largely driven by strong customer connections and the company's robust omnichannel infrastructure, Silverman says. Fitch is expecting retail choppiness throughout 2025 as consumer sentiment moderates, particularly for lower-income customers, and headwinds like tariffs put pressure on prices, though Walmart should be able to manage the unpredictability, Silverman says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1513 ET - Some 55% more U.S. farmers declared bankruptcy in 2024 versus the previous year, although the number is still relatively low versus what was seen in the previous decade. In 2024, 216 farm bankruptcy cases were filed, says the American Farm Bureau Federation in a note, up from 139 bankruptcies in 2023. 1326 ET - St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem, a voter on the central bank's policy committee this year, says monetary policy is less restrictive than it was before last year's rate cuts, but is still working to restrain the economy modestly. Speaking at an Economic Club of New York meeting in Manhattan, Musalem says that it will be appropriate to keep policy restrictive if inflation stays above the Fed's 2% target. On the other hand, lower rates could be the right move is the labor market worsens and inflation expectations stay anchored, he adds. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1116 ET - U.S. median home-sale prices rose 3.7% year over year in the four weeks ending Feb. 16, the smallest gain since September, Redfin says. Mortgage rates fell to 6.87%, the year's lowest, easing costs slightly. Inventory rose to five months' supply, up from 4.1 last year, the highest since early 2019, as listings increase and sales slow. New listings jumped 4.2%, hitting a three-year peak. Homes sell for 2% below asking price--the biggest discount in two years--and take 57 days to go under contract, the longest in five. Buyers gained negotiating power, especially in buyer-friendly markets like coastal Florida, though West Coast and Northeast sellers still dominate. With home-touring up, spring may shift dynamics. Updated, well-priced homes in top areas will sell quickly, often above asking, while others linger, giving buyers leverage. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1108 ET - Inflows into euro investment-grade credit funds decelerated for four consecutive weeks through Feb. 12, CreditSights says in a note. Average daily inflows have slowed to $102 million, versus the daily average so far this year of $260 million. Short-dated funds garnered the most inflows, while intermediate-dated funds recorded outflows. Flows into euro high-yield funds have been positive for four weeks running, with average daily inflows of $40 million in the week to Feb. 12, almost double the daily average in 2025 so far, CreditSights says. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

1050 ET - U.S. Treasury debt rallies after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent says that the Treasury won 't be in a hurry to auction more longer-term securities. "That's a long way off, and we're going to see what the market wants," Bessent said in an interview with Bloomberg News. Some bond investors have been concerned that the U.S. could turn to issuing more higher-duration notes and bonds at the same time that the Fed is continuing to reduce its balance-sheet holdings, potentially creating a supply glut. Bessent's comments help ease those concerns, at least in the near term. The 10-year Treasury note recently trades around a 4.508% yield, compared with 4.535% at Wednesday's close. The 2-year note touches a yield of 4.259%, compared with 4.272% Wednesday.(matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1045 ET - U.S. home values rose $2.5 trillion in 2024, reaching $49.7 trillion, a 5.2% increase--the slowest since 2019--according to Redfin. More homes are for sale, creating buyer's markets in many areas, yet prices continue to climb monthly. Redfin predicts steady price growth in 2025 due to buyer competition over limited listings compared to pre-pandemic levels. Over the past decade, U.S. home values have more than doubled from $23 trillion in 2014, peaking at $50.4 trillion in July 2024. The slight drop reflects seasonal trends, with values rising March to September and dipping in winter. New construction is also boosting market valuation. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1039 ET - Inflows into U.S. investment-grade corporate bonds reached a three-month high in the week to Feb. 12 after strengthening over five consecutive weeks, CreditSights analysts say in a note. Inflows were driven by flows into exchange-traded funds. U.S. investment-grade funds posted average daily inflows of $1.7 billion, with daily inflows into ETFs covering this asset class at $1.5 billion. This eclipsed the trailing four-week daily average of $1.2 billion. U.S. high-yield credit funds extended their recent streak of inflows to six consecutive weeks, even as the pace of demand slowed modestly, the analysts say. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

1025 ET - The European Commission wants to hear from business leaders to help it work out which of the European Union's regulations need simplifying and how, says Valdis Dombrovskis, the EU's economy commissioner. Companies can help policymakers by "providing precise input when we are discussing different pieces of legislation: what is working, what is not, where there are overlaps, where there are gaps, where there are inconsistencies," he says. Dombrovskis' comments come as the commission tries to streamline its knot of regulations to make it easier for businesses to comply with them and attract investment into the bloc. Dombrovskis also notes that each commissioner has been tasked with setting up dialogues with industry to work on ways to simplify rules in their own policy area. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

1019 ET - U.K. gilt-yield curves could steepen as short-dated yields fall on expectations of the Bank of England cutting interest rates further, albeit gradually, ING's Benjamin Schroeder and Michiel Tukker say in a note. After this week's stronger-than-expected U.K. inflation data, a March rate cut looks unlikely. However, markets price a good chance of a reduction in May. A weak economy could also lead to accelerated rate cuts. The rates strategists favor short-dated gilts but say trends in U.S. Treasurys are more likely to influence longer-dated gilts. "The next move will likely be a steepening of curves," they say. Two-year gilt yields edge slightly lower to 4.256% while 30-year yields are steady at 5.190%, Tradeweb data show. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

1002 ET - In a period of intense economic and geopolitical challenges, Europe badly needs leadership and initiative from its largest economy, Germany, says Bank Syz's Adrien Pichoud in a note. Germany will hold elections on Sunday. Talks to form a coalition usually last several weeks or months in Germany after an election, the chief economist says. "But the sooner a new government will be formed, the faster Germany will be able to lead Europe's efforts to address its geopolitical and economic challenges," he says. The centre-right CDU/CSU is expected to win, while among the mainstream parties, SPD and the Greens should make into the Bundestag, alongside the extreme-right AfD. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

VOLUNTARY PUBLIC EXCHANGE OFFER LAUNCHED BY UNICREDIT S.P.A. ON ALL ORDINARY SHARES OF BANCO BPM S

PRESS RELEASE

Milan, 20 February 2025 - With reference to the notice pursuant to art. 102, paragraph 1 of the

Italian Consolidated Financial Act and art. 37 of the Issuers’ Regulation published on 25 November

2024 in relation to the promotion of a voluntary public exchange offer pursuant to and for the

purposes of artt.102 and 106, paragraph 4, of the Italian Consolidated Financial Act (the

“Offer”) for all of the ordinary shares of Banco BPM S.p.A.(“BPM”), the Board of Directors

of UniCredit S.p.A., held on 20 February 2025, resolved, inter alia, to convene the shareholders

meeting in Milan, on 27 March 2025(previously convened on 10 April 2025), in single call, in order

to resolve in the extraordinary session upon, inter alia, the proposal to grant the Board of

Directors, pursuant to art. 2443 of the Italian Civil Code, with the power, to be exercised within

31 December 2025, to increase the share capital, in one or more tranches and in a divisible form,

without pre-emption right pursuant to art. 2441, paragraph 4, first sentence, of the Italian Civil

Code, and with issuance of maximum no. 278,000,000[1] ordinary shares, with ordinary rights and the

same characteristics as the shares already outstanding on the issue date, whose issuance price shall

be determined by the Board of Directors pursuant to applicable laws, to be paid up by way of

contribution in kind since it its functional to the Offer.

This press release is disclosed pursuant to art. 36 of the Issuers’ Regulation.

The notice of call shall be published with the timing and modalities required by the applicable

regulations.

[1] Considering the dividend distribution proposals for the 2024 financial year recently approved

by the Boards of Directors of UniCredit and BPM, and not yet approved by their respective

shareholders' meetings, the maximum number of UniCredit shares to be issued in connection with the

Offer has been increased from 266,000,000 to 278,000,000 only to ensure capacity in all theoretical

scenarios of the adjustment of the Consideration (as indicated in the Offer Notice) that may arise

as a result of a potential misalignment in the payment of dividends by UniCredit and/or BPM before

the completion of the Offer.

data.title: Crombie REITs Brief: National Bank Notes "Top-end of range expected for organic growth, future distribution increases likely"
Ross Ulbricht, Silk Road founder pardoned by Trump, calls to ‘free’ early Bitcoin evangelist Roger Ver
data.description:
Ross Ulbricht, the Silk Road founder whom President Trump recently pardoned, is joining the call to “free” early Bitcoin evangelist Roger Ver. 

“Roger Ver was there for me when I was down and needed help. Now Roger needs our support,” Ulbricht said on X. “No one should spend the rest of their life in prison over taxes.”

The move is part of the crypto industry’s continuing reliance on President Trump — a vocal crypto supporter who sees himself as a victim of weaponized “lawfare” — to achieve political ends.

In their motion to dismiss the case, Ver’s white-shoe lawyers from Steptoe LLP and Kimura London & White argued the Department of Justice’s case was politically motivated due to Ver’s libertarian and crypto advocacy and represented “unconstitutional overreach.”

Ver, an early adopter and promoter of Bitcoin who was once more commonly known as “Bitcoin Jesus,” was arrested in Spain while attending a crypto conference at the request of the U.S. Department of Justice in April 2024. 

The U.S. indicted him on multiple charges of mail fraud, tax evasion and filing false tax returns, alleging he owed approximately $48 million in taxes related to his exit from the U.S. in 2014 when he renounced his citizenship and subsequent sales of bitcoin. Bitcoin traded within a wide range that year, peaking above $800 and cratering to around $330 by the end of the year, according to The Block’s data. 

According to the eight-count indictment, the DOJ accused Ver, a former California resident, of undervaluing the sale of tens of thousands of bitcoins worth $240 million in 2017, technically owned by his U.S.-based companies, MemoryDealers and Agilestar. 

“Even though Ver was not then a U.S. citizen, he was still legally required to report to the IRS and pay tax on certain distributions such as dividends from MemoryDealers and Agilestar, which were U.S. corporations,” the U.S. Attorney's Office of the Central District of California, wrote after his detainment in Spain.

The U.S. government also claims Ver undervalued those companies, which sold computer and networking equipment and at times held upwards of 73,000 bitcoins, when preparing his “exit tax” during the expatriation process. 

Ver was released on €150,000 (around $157,000) bail last May but remains under house arrest in Spain while awaiting an extradition decision. He faces up to 109 years in prison if convicted — a sentence his supporters argue is wildly disproportionate.

From 'Free Ross' to 'Free Roger'

While the so-called “Free Roger” campaign, modeled after the successful “Free Ross” campaign, has achieved a degree of grassroots support and backing from several prominent individuals — primarily in crypto and libertarian circles — it is also a tough sell for many. 

Notably, Ulbricht was actually — not theoretically — serving two life sentences at the time, which many viewed as an excessive attempt at deterrence and retribution. His darknet drug market has also been deemed the first use case for bitcoin, proving the cryptocurrency worked (more or less) as advertised and kickstarted the process of wider adoption.

A presidential pardon was also the last remaining option for Ulbricht and his mother, Lynn, who spent the decade of his incarceration exploring all legal options available for an early release from prison.

Comparatively, although Ver helped many crypto startups in the early days, including by seeding BitPay and Kraken with capital, he has also spurned many would-be supporters, most notably when supporting the “big blocks” camp during the so-called “Blocksize Wars” that led to a contentious forking of Bitcoin and Bitcoin Cash. 

This is not Ver’s first brush with the law. In 2002, he served a 10-month sentence in federal prison for selling explosives — specifically fireworks — on eBay without a proper license, illegally storing them and mailing them improperly. On his website, Ver refers to this case as “a unique legal challenge,” claiming he was “the only person ever prosecuted” for such a crime. After completing his prison term, he was placed on supervised release for three years.

Following his supervised release, Ver left the United States in 2005 and relocated to Japan. He later sought citizenship in Guatemala but encountered issues after being misled by individuals who promised citizenship through improper channels. Ultimately, Ver obtained citizenship in St. Kitts and Nevis in 2014 and subsequently renounced his U.S. citizenship.

Ver argues the U.S. government has overstepped its legal authority while building this recent case against him. In 2018, for instance, IRS agents interrogated his tax lawyer and MemoryDealers employees without a warrant. The Free Roger team alleges this was a breach of attorney-client privilege and that the IRS is ignoring evidence Ver attempted in good faith to meet his tax obligations. (Notably, Ver’s website seemingly confirms a long-suspected rumor that Ver was involved, though unnamed, in a 2022 Supreme Court case regarding client confidentiality that was ultimately dismissed in 2023.)

Ver’s lawyers also argue the very notion of an “exit tax” — implemented in 2008 to mitigate tax avoidance and evasion by ensuring that wealthy individuals pay taxes on unrealized capital gains before leaving the U.S. tax system by renouncing their citizenship — is unconstitutional, in part due to the vagaries of how it would have applied to Ver’s crypto holdings at the time. 

“After six years of investigation, the IRS charges Roger under questionable pretenses, suggesting he renounced his citizenship to avoid taxes, contradicting his proactive compliance efforts,” Ver’s website reads. 

According to Polymarket, Ver has a 10% chance of being pardoned by Trump within his first 100 days in office, putting him just below whistleblowers Edward Snowden and Julian Assange. Meanwhile, bettors are giving FTX founder Sam Bankman-Fried, whose parents are also reportedly seeking a clemency deal from Trump for their son, 2% odds.

data.title: Abraham, Fruchter & Twersky LLP Notifies Shareholders of a Class Action Lawsuit on Behalf of Investors who Acquired Shares of Block, Inc. (NYSE: XYZ)
data.description:
NEW YORK, Feb. 20, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Abraham, Fruchter & Twersky, LLP (www.aftlaw.com), a nationally recognized securities fraud class action law firm announces that purchasers of Block, Inc. Class A common stock between February 26, 2020 and April 30, 2024, inclusive (the “Class Period”), have until March 18, 2025 to seek appointment as lead plaintiff of the Block securities fraud class action captioned Gonsalves v. Block, Inc., No. 25-00642 (N.D. Cal.).

CASE ALLEGATIONS: Block owns Square, a financial services platform for small and medium-sized businesses and Cash App (f/k/a “Square Cash”), a mobile payment service that allows users to transfer money using a mobile phone.

The Block class action alleges that defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (i) Block had engaged in widespread and years-long compliance lapses at Square and Cash App, including by failing to conduct basic due diligence regarding its customers’ identities or the nature of customer transactions so as to prevent the platforms from being used for illegal or illicit activities; (ii) Block had effectively created a haven for widespread illegal and illicit activities on its Square and Cash App platforms by imposing minimal obligations on customers seeking to open accounts, transact, and/or deposit or withdraw funds; encouraging the use of bitcoin; and pressuring Block’s banking partners to forgo ordinary know your customer due diligence activities; (iii) thousands of transactions on Square and Cash App were made in connection with a wide variety of illegal and illicit activities, including, inter alia, money laundering, child sexual abuse, sex trafficking, drug trafficking, terrorism financing, contract killings, and illicit payments to entities and persons subject to economic sanctions; (iv) Block allowed its customers to withdraw funds even after the accounts had been flagged for potentially illegal or illicit activities; (v) Block customers could open up multiple accounts using fake identities in order to engage in illegal or illicit activities; (vi) Block’s senior leadership and the Board of Directors had failed to correct identified compliance deficiencies despite numerous red flags, internal employee reports of deficiencies, and customer complaints; (vii) Block’s Cash App user metrics had been artificially inflated through the use of fake accounts and the ability of criminals and other bad actors to open multiple accounts; and (viii) as a result of the above, Block was subject to a material, undisclosed risk of its conduct being exposed, thereby exposing Block to reputational harm, adverse regulatory actions, the loss of business activity, and adverse impacts to Block’s operations and financial results.

On March 23, 2023, Hindenburg Research published a damaging exposé on Block titled: “Block: How Inflated User Metrics and ‘Frictionless’ Fraud Facilitation Enabled Insiders To Cash Out Over $1 Billion.” On August 3, 2023, Block disclosed that the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission and the U.S. Department of Justice were investigating the allegations against Block and its employees contained in the Hindenburg Research report. On February 16, 2024, NBC News reported that federal regulators were probing allegations by two whistleblowers that Cash App performed inadequate due diligence on its users – including “‘no effective procedure to establish the[ir] identity.’” Finally, on May 1, 2024, NBC News reported that federal prosecutors were investigating Block for failing to conduct basic due diligence on its customers, and processing thousands of transactions involving countries subject to economic sanctions and multiple cryptocurrency transactions for terrorist groups.

LEAD PLAINTIFF DEADLINE: Lead plaintiff motions for the Block class action must be filed with the court no later than March 18, 2025. If you suffered substantial losses by investing in Block securities and would like to discuss serving as lead plaintiff of the Block class action lawsuit, please contact Jack Fruchter by email at block@aftlaw.com. You can also reach Jack Fruchter by telephone at (212) 279-5050. There is no cost or obligation to you.

Abraham, Fruchter & Twersky, LLP (www.aftlaw.com), is a law firm based in New York with an office in California that has extensive experience in litigating securities law violations on behalf of investors.

data.title: ICE canola futures rise on vegoil optimism
data.description:
ICE canola futures rose Thursday on a day that saw resumed strength in soyoil.

• Most-traded May canola (RSK5) settled up $6.10 at $680.10 per metric ton. Other contract months had similar but slightly stronger gains.

• The Canadian dollar rose and stayed at a multi-month high and resistance level.

data.title: Update: Diversified Energy Shares Fall After Share Offering Priced
data.description:
(Updates with stock price movement in the headline and first paragraph.)

Diversified Energy shares fell 4.4% in recent Thursday trading, a day after the company priced an underwritten public offering of 8.5 million shares at $14.50 apiece for gross proceeds of $123.3 million.

The company granted the underwriters a 30-day option to buy up to an additional 850,000 shares. The offering is expected to settle on Friday.

The company plans to use the net proceeds to repay a portion of the debt in connection with its proposed acquisition of Maverick Natural Resources. If the acquisition doesn't close, the proceeds will be used for other debt and general corporate purposes.

data.title: Mineros Up 2% As Issues Dividend Distribution Proposal
data.description:
Mineros was up 2% at last look late in Thursday's trading session after announcing its board of directors has proposed for the approval of shareholders that a quarterly ordinary dividend be paid of US$0.025 per share. This represents a total distribution of $0.10 per share per annum, or near $30 million in total for the year, calculated based on the number of shares issued and outstanding as of the close of business on Dec. 31, 2024.

This represents the same dividend payout as that approved for 2024.

The dividend proposal represents a distribution of 35% of the company's profit for the year, which is in line with the company's dividend policy, which is to pay in dividends at least 15% of the net income of the prior fiscal year.

data.title: Market Chatter: CoStar Readies Bid to Acquire Domain
data.description:
CoStar is reportedly planning a bid worth 2.7 billion Australian dollars ($1.72 billion) for Australian property listings company Domain, Reuters reported Thursday, citing sources with knowledge of the matter.

Domain is roughly 60% owned by Nine Entertainment, an Australian media group, the media outlet reported.

CoStar has purchased up to 19% of Domain's shares at 4.20 Australian dollars each, with intentions to acquire the entire company at the same price, Reuters said.

The proposed price is a 34.6% premium to Domain's Thursday closing share price of 3.12 Australian dollars, according to Reuters.

CoStar, Domain and Nine Entertainment did not immediately respond to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

data.title: CBOT wheat ends down as concerns ease over cold US weather
data.description:
Chicago Board of Trade wheat futures settled lower on Thursday as concerns eased among traders about the risk for damage to U.S. crops from frigid weather, analysts said.

  • Most of the US winter wheat crop has an adequate layer of snow to protect it from icy temperatures, according to a daily US Department of Agriculture weather report.

  • Temperatures are also expected to rise in the coming days after a cold spell, traders said.

  • The International Grains Council raised its outlook for the world's 2024/25 wheat harvest by 1 million tons to 797 million tons.

  • CBOT March soft red winter wheat (WH25) dipped 6-1/2 cents to close at $5.85-1/2 per bushel. The market pulled back after the most-active contract touched an eight-month high on Wednesday.

  • K.C. March hard red winter wheat (KWH25) ended 6-1/4 cents lower at $6.07-1/2 per bushel, and Minneapolis March spring wheat (MWEH25) slipped 3/4-cent to $6.32-3/4 per bushel.

  • Agricultural lender CoBank projected US 2025 plantings of spring wheat other than durum at 10.0 million acres, down 5.9% from 10.63 million in 2024.

data.title: Domino's Pizza's Q4 Likely to Come in Slightly Below Estimates, RBC Says
data.description:
Domino's Pizza is likely to report Q4 results slightly below estimates while initiating 2025 outlook in line with expectations, RBC Capital Markets said in an earnings preview emailed Thursday.

RBC said it would be looking for US demand trends and the possibility of a partnership with DoorDash heading into the earnings set to be released Monday.

The firm lowered its Q4 US same-store sales estimate to 1% on softer category growth which compares to the consensus of 1.3%.

Despite revising its estimate, RBC said it was "constructive" going into the earnings due to the potential of a DoorDash partnership announcement and a more positive tone on international business.

Domino's current exclusivity agreement with Uber is set to expire at the end of Q1, and the company could either announce a renewal or additional partnership with DoorDash starting in Q2, according to the note.

A renewal with Uber would be a "negative" as investors will likely take that as a signal of softer demand given that DoorDash's market share is three times larger than Uber's, according to RBC.

RBC has an outperform rating on the stock with a price target of $500.

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1501 ET - U.S. natural gas futures snap a seven-session winning streak as near-term forecasts point to milder weather to follow this week's Arctic blast. "Severe cold weather brings prices up and warming trends take prices right back down," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. This week's cold spell had more than 130 million people under wind chills in single digits or lower, he adds. The Nymex front month settles down 3% at $4.152/mmBtu in volatile trade that saw a swing between an overnight high of $4.476 and the day's low of $4.034. The EIA's storage report showed the inventory deficit versus the five-year average increasing to 118 Bcf last week from 67 Bcf a week earlier. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1410 ET - Innergex Renewable had a standout quarter, ending the year on a strong note thanks to the wind segment. Scotiabank's Robert Hope says in a research report that Ebitda proportionate came to C$210 million, topping consensus estimates of C$180 million. "The Wind segment was the key driver of the beat, though it appears that the majority of the beat was driven by one-time items," Hope says. For the year ahead, management expectations includes Ebitda proportionate of C$825 million-C$875 million and distributable cash flow per share of C$0.75-C$0.95. "Our EBITDA estimate of C$868 million is towards the top end of the range, while our distributable cash flow estimate of C$0.80 is in the bottom half of the range," the analyst says. Shares are up 8.6% to C$8.36.(adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1254 ET - Mexico's reliance on U.S. piped gas will continue to grow, Fitch Ratings says, even as uncertainties over bilateral trade relations increase. U.S. gas imports provide reliable and cost-effective energy for Mexico, buoyed by rising demand, constrained domestic production, and pipeline infrastructure expansion, Fitch says. The market share of U.S. piped gas in Mexico is expected to increase in the coming years, according to Fitch. Trade uncertainties have emerged since President Trump came into office, but the administration has mainly focused on Mexico's exports to the U.S., and Fitch says it sees policy-driven disruptions to the gas trade as a smaller risk. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1127 ET - Canada's oil producers are likely to prove resilient in the face of President Trump's threatened 10% tariff on all U.S.-bound Canadian energy exports, S&P Global Ratings says. It notes many U.S. refiners rely on Canadian crude imports as refining feedstock, though most Canadian oil and gas companies have limited alternative sale markets and lack sufficient domestic refining capacity. S&P expects the financial burden of tariffs would be shared by U.S. refiners, and ultimately American consumers, and Canadian producers. And it adds the relatively weak Canadian dollar will limit top-line effects for Canadian producers, while the industry's low debt leverage will further support credit metric resilience and ratings. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1115 ET - A swift return of Russian oil flows to Europe in the event of a peace deal between the Kremlin and Ukraine remains uncertain, according to Rystad Energy. "Gas markets saw diverging price movements this week, with expectations of a swift comeback of Russian gas driving declines in Europe and Asia," says senior analyst Christoph Halser. European natural-gas prices have been trading below the 50-euros-a-megawatt-hour mark in recent days on hopes that talks between the U.S. and Russia could lead to a lifting of Western sanctions. However, the path to a peace deal looks complex and uncertain, with European leaders and Ukrainian officials currently excluded from negotiations. "While a declared joint target for a Ukraine peace deal may open the door for Russian gas, the feasibility of a quick return remains questionable," Halser says. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1101 ET - U.S. natural gas inventories fell by 196 billion cubic feet last week to 2,101 Bcf, the EIA reports. The drawdown widened the deficit against the five-year average to 118 Bcf from 67 Bcf the week before, and inventories were 386 Bcfbelow their year-earlier level. Last week's storage draw was bigger than the average 187 Bcf forecast in a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts, and at the high end of estimates that ranged from draws of 176 Bcf to 200 Bcf. Nymex are lower after seven straight sessions of gains, with the front month off 3.8% at $4.116/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0934 ET - Reduced oil supply from Kazakhstan and production shut-ins due to cold weather in North Dakota support oil futures, which are higher in early trade. "Add in OPEC+ members stating further delays are likely in its April production increase and the crude price positives near term seem to be outweighing the negatives," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler says in a note. Verbal conflicts between President Trump and Ukrainian President Zelensky could delay a Russian peace deal, he adds. WTI is up 0.7% at $72.74 a barrel ahead of today's March expiration. Brent is up 0.7% at $76.60 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0922 ET - Natural gas futures are lower following a seven-day weather-driven rally as near-term forecasts turn a bit milder following this week's Arctic blast. The EIA's weekly inventory report is expected to show another large storage withdrawal, widening the deficit over the year-earlier level and the five-year average. The colder-than-normal weather could push the deficit beyond 300 Bcf, which will remain a bullish factor even as the heating cycle winds down, "capable of keeping this market well supported throughout the shoulder period," Ritterbusch says in a note. The Nymex front month is down 3.6% at $4.127/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0833 ET - A peace deal between Russia and Ukraine would impact natural gas much more than other commodities, according to MUFG analysts. "Should a potential peace agreement include higher Russian pipeline natural gas flows via Ukraine to Europe, then this would make it easier for European gas storage to refill ahead of the next winter season," Ehsan Khoman and Soojin Kim say. Increased supply over the summer would put pressure on TTF gas prices and fill European storage swiftly, according to the financial group. MUFG forecasts the benchmark TTF contract at 38 euros a megawatt hour this year but said it could fall below 30 euros on potential storage congestion risks. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0811 ET - Oil prices move back into positive territory in afternoon trade, supported by supply-side concerns. Brent crude is up 0.5% at $76.39 a barrel, while WTI rises 0.4% to $72.40 a barrel after sliding earlier in the session. Both benchmarks are boosted by disruptions to Kazakhstan's oil flows, the possibility that OPEC+ could further delay its output hike plans and reports that the Group of Seven is considering tightening the Russian oil cap, according to analysts at MUFG. Meanwhile, any peace agreement between Russia and Ukraine is unlikely to lead to an increase in the Kremlin's oil flows due to limits imposed by OPEC+'s production quotas. "There is more uncertainty in global oil markets this year than in any year since the pandemic," the analysts say. MUFG forecasts Brent at an average of $73 a barrel this year. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0715 ET - European natural-gas prices continue to trade below the 50-euros-a-megawatt-hour mark amid warmer weather forecasts and EU talks to relax storage targets. "Warmer than normal temperatures are expected by the end of the week, which should dent gas demand," DNB Markets analysts say. Meanwhile, imports of LNG should continue to be high in the coming weeks. "It is very much needed for Europe to get through the heating season and rebuild inventories into next year," they say. EU storage levels are only 42.6% full, according to data from Gas Infrastructure Europe, but the European Commission is reportedly set to work with member states to allow for more flexible refilling requirements. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract trades 0.6% lower at 47.96 euros a megawatt hour. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0549 ET - Schneider Electric posted an encouraging set of results overall, Morgan Stanley analyst Max Yates says in a research note. The French energy-management and automation group gave impressive numbers, with the expectation for the share price to increase by a mid-single digit percentage, Yates says. However, "we would argue continued consensus upgrades will be harder to come by in the next 12-18 months as supply and demand in some of the medium voltage market balances out and with Schneider already at record high Ebita margins," he says. Shares trade 5.5% higher at 260.85 euros. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

data.title: US dollar stumbles on 'tariff fatigue'; yen soars as BOJ hike bets rise
data.description:
Key points:
  • Euro gains after three straight days of losses
  • Yen surges to 11-week highs vs dollar
  • Traders monitor geopolitics; euro most sensitive to Russia talks
  • US jobless claims rise in latest week
  • Philly Fed index drops in February

The U.S. dollar dropped against major currencies on Thursday, as investors took a step back and assessed President Donald Trump's latest tariff plans, while the yen rose to 11-week peaks versus the greenback as bets increased for further rate hikes by the Bank of Japan (BOJ).

U.S. data showing initial jobless claims, which were in line with expectations, and a report indicating factory output growth slowed in the mid-Atlantic region in February had minimal impact on the currency market. The reports have not changed expectations that the Federal Reserve will remain on hold for several months.

Tariffs, however, remained a major focus for currency investors even though their impact has weakened for now.

Trump said on Wednesday he will announce fresh tariffs over the next month or sooner, adding lumber and forest products to previously announced plans to impose duties on imported cars, semiconductors and pharmaceuticals.

"The market has reached tariff-fatigue where it's just not reacting...as it did right after the election in November, December and even in January," said Paresh Upadhyaya, director of fixed income and currency strategy, at Amundi U.S. in Boston.

"It's now operating using the government shutdown playbook, where everyone thinks they have seen this dog-and-pony show before and expects the same thing. There is the threat of tariffs, leading to negotiations and a reprieve and some form of resolution."

In afternoon trading, the euro rose 0.7% against the dollar to $1.0499 , rising after three straight days of losses, while the greenback slid 0.7% versus the Swiss franc to 0.8979 franc .

The dollar earlier slipped after data showed initial claims for state unemployment benefits rose 5,000 to a seasonally adjusted 219,000 for the week ended February 15. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast 215,000 claims for that week.

Also marginally weighing on the dollar was a separate report indicating the Philadelphia Fed's monthly manufacturing index tumbled by 26.2 points - the most in nearly five years - to 18.1 in February from 44.3 in January.

"The U.S. economic momentum is slowing...and not providing a supportive backdrop to the dollar," said Boris Kovacevic, global macro strategist at payments firm Convera in Vienna. "The Fed's hawkish monetary policy...can only do so much to help the greenback and the pause is already fully priced in."

The yen, meanwhile, rose to an 11-week peak of 149.40 per dollar. The U.S. currency last traded down 1.1% at 149.77 yen, driven mostly by safe-haven buying amid worries about Trump's tariffs and rising expectations of more BOJ hikes this year. (0#JPYIRPR)

The euro also dropped against the yen to a more than one-week low, last down 0.5% at 157.21 yen .

BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda said on Thursday he met Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba for a regular exchange of views on the economy and financial markets, suggesting that the government has no objections to hiking rates and normalizing policy.

"We have been early and patient with the bullish call on the yen and are sticking with it - the currency should be far closer to 120 or 130 yen," wrote David Rosenberg, founder and president of research firm Rosenberg Research.

Markets are also monitoring geopolitical developments after Trump and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy traded barbs on Wednesday, with the U.S. president calling the latter "a dictator".

Zelenskiy on Thursday, however, took a more constructive tone, saying Ukraine is ready to work quickly and tirelessly to produce a strong and useful agreement on investments and security with the United States. He said on social media site X that he had a "good discussion" with Keith Kellogg, the U.S. envoy for Ukraine and Russia.

The euro, the most sensitive currency to Russia-Ukraine news, hit session highs against the dollar after the Zelenskiy headlines.

Investors also weighed Trump's comments saying "it's possible" for the United States and China to have a new trade deal. He expects Chinese President Xi Jinping to visit the U.S., without saying when.

The Australian and New Zealand dollars, whose countries have huge exposure to Chinese trade, gained on the China headlines.

The Aussie dollar last traded up 0.9% at US$0.6401, also reacting to a mixed jobs report showing employment outpaced forecasts for a second successive month in January, yet the unemployment rate still ticked higher.

The New Zealand dollar also posted sharp gains, rising 1% to US$0.5764.

Sterling was also up, gaining 0.6% against the dollar to $1.2664.

data.title: NZX Ltd.: Final Dividend of NZ$0.031 per Share
Wingstop Shares Hit 52-Week Low After Analysts Cut Price Targets
data.description:
By Josh Beckerman

Wingstop shares declined after several analysts cut their price targets for the stock a day after the company reported earnings.

The stock fell to a 52-week low of $243.03 on Thursday. Shares recovered in afternoon trading, but were remained down 7.1%, at $246.31. This year shares have fallen 13%.

Morgan Stanley reduced its target to $375 from $389, while UBS lowered its price to $300 from $330. Barclays, Wedbush and TD Securities also lowered their targets.

Benchmark, which changed its target to $325 from $340, mentioned investor fears of deceleration in same-store sales growth, but said various factors like franchisee growth and technology investment "all paint a picture of a category and industry leader who should only increasingly benefit from scale advantages."

On Wednesday morning, Wingstop said revenue rose 27%, to $161.8 million. Revenue was below Wall Street forecasts, while earnings were higher than analysts expected, according to FactSet. Wingstop shares closed down 13% Wednesday.

Write to Josh Beckerman at josh.beckerman@wsj.com

data.title: SATS ASA: Mandatory notification of trade
data.description:
Reference is made to the stock exchange announcements published by SATS ASA (the "Company") on 20 December 2024 and 14 February 2025, regarding the pending liquidation of SATS Management Invest AS and settlement of loans granted by HFN Group AS to SATS Management Invest AS and certain shareholders in SATS Management Invest AS. SATS Management Invest AS is a share purchase program that was implemented prior to the Company's IPO in 2019 during its prior ownership.

HFN Group AS and Gravir Invest AS, a wholly-owned company of Sondre Gravir, CEO of SATS ASA, have, as part of the announced liquidation, agreed to settle certain loans incl. accrued interest granted in connection with Gravir's investment in SATS Management Investment AS prior to the IPO by way of delivery of 292,759 shares in SATS Management Investment AS. The details are further set out in the attached notification form.

Sondre Gravir will continue to directly and indirectly hold 1,326,237 shares in SATS ASA following the transaction, including the 128,514 shares allocated to him in connection with the Share Investment Program announced on 14 February 2025.

Gravir Invest AS is a closely associated entity of Sondre Gravir.

This information is subject to the disclosure requirements pursuant to article 19 of the EU Market Abuse Regulation and section 5-12 of the Norwegian Securities Trading Act.

data.title: Matthews International Recommended Director Nominees Elected by Shareholders
data.description:
Matthews International said Thursday that shareholders voted to elect all three of its recommended director nominees at the 2025 annual meeting, based on preliminary results.

The company nominated Terry Dunlap, Alvaro Garcia-Tunon and J. Michael Nauman. The slate was challenged by activist investor Barrington Capital Group, who in a proxy fight recommended its own nominees, Ana Amicarella, Chan Galbato and James Mitarotonda.

The final results are expected to be provided in a Form 8-K, the company said in a press release.
